skillName: Write integers in words and figures
misconceptionSummary: Students write words to figures incorrectly by adding extra zeros or misplacing digit groups.

steps.narration: Both of these wrong answers come from the same problem — not knowing how many digits belong in each group. The right answer has a fixed structure.

visuals.ruleText: After the thousands comma, there are always exactly 3 digits
visuals.subText: 42,016 — the last group is 016, not 16 or 160

steps.narration: The group after the comma always has three digits. If the words give you a remainder under a hundred, pad it with a zero on the left. Sixteen becomes zero-one-six.
Forty-two goes to the left of the comma. Sixteen needs three digits on the right — so it becomes zero-one-six. Watch how the zero fills the hundreds slot.
Forty-two thousand and sixteen. Six digits total — two before the comma, three after, with a zero holding the hundreds place.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: writing 4,2016 or 42,160 instead of 42,016. The group after the comma always needs exactly three digits — pad with zeros if needed.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Always ask yourself: do I have exactly three digits after the comma? If not, add zeros to the left of the remainder until you do.
